tracks.name: Linux Fundamentals
tracks.description: Master the core text-processing tools every Linux user needs.

lessons.title: grep: Search Like a Pro
lessons.introduction: grep (Global Regular Expression Print) is one of the most powerful text search tools in Linux. It searches for patterns in files and streams, making it indispensable for log analysis, code review, and data extraction.
lessons.purpose: Search for patterns in text files and streams

sections.title: Basic Search
sections.content: The simplest use of grep is searching for a literal string in a file.
sections.explanation: Prints every line in server.log that contains the word ERROR

sections.title: Case-Insensitive Search
sections.content: Use -i to ignore case differences.
sections.explanation: Matches ERROR, error, Error, eRrOr — all of them

sections.title: Show Line Numbers
sections.content: Use -n to display line numbers of matches.
sections.explanation: Shows exactly where critical events occur in your log

sections.title: Count Matches
sections.content: Use -c to count matching lines instead of printing them.
sections.explanation: Returns a single count — great for quick health checks

sections.title: Invert Match
sections.content: Use -v to show lines that do NOT match.
sections.explanation: Strips comment lines from config files or logs

sections.title: Extended Regex
sections.content: Use -E to enable | (OR), + and ? quantifiers.
sections.explanation: Matches any of the three severity levels in one pass

sections.title: Context Lines
sections.content: Use -A, -B, -C to show surrounding context lines.
sections.explanation: Shows 3 lines before and after each FATAL — vital for debugging

sections.title: Extract Only Match
sections.content: Use -o to print only the matched portion, not the whole line.
sections.explanation: Extracts just the IP addresses — nothing else

common_mistakes: Forgetting -E for extended regex (| and + won't work without it)
Using grep on binary files — use grep -a to treat as text
Case sensitivity — always consider -i if unsure
best_practices: Chain grep calls: grep 'ERROR' log | grep -v 'test' to narrow results
Use --color=auto for readable highlighted output
Combine -l (list files) with -r for codebase-wide searches

exercises.prompt: Count how many ERROR lines are in server.log
exercises.hint: Use -c flag

exercises.prompt: Show all lines NOT containing 'INFO' in server.log
exercises.hint: Invert with -v

exercises.prompt: Extract only IP addresses from apache.log
exercises.hint: Use -oE with an IP regex pattern

quiz.question: Which grep flag makes the search case-insensitive?
quiz.explanation: -i stands for ignore-case. -c counts, -v inverts, -n shows line numbers.

quiz.question: How do you count matching lines with grep?
quiz.explanation: -c (count) returns only the number of matching lines.

quiz.question: To search for 'error' OR 'warning' in a file, you need flag ___
quiz.explanation: -E enables extended regular expressions which support the | alternation operator.

lessons.title: sed: Stream Editing Mastery
lessons.introduction: sed (Stream EDitor) processes text line by line, applying editing commands. It's perfect for find-and-replace, deletion, insertion, and complex text transformations — without opening a text editor.
lessons.purpose: Edit text streams non-interactively — substitute, delete, insert

sections.title: Basic Substitution
sections.content: The s command substitutes text: s/OLD/NEW/ replaces first match per line, s/OLD/NEW/g replaces all.
sections.explanation: Replaces every occurrence of ERROR with ALERT in every line

sections.title: Delete Lines
sections.content: The d command deletes lines matching a pattern.
sections.explanation: Removes all comment lines (starting with #)

sections.title: Print Specific Lines
sections.content: Use -n with p to print only specified lines.
sections.explanation: Prints only lines 10 through 20 — like a precise head/tail

sections.title: In-Place Editing
sections.content: Use -i to edit files in-place (WARNING: destructive!).
sections.explanation: Modifies the actual file. Use -i.bak to keep a backup.

sections.title: Address Ranges
sections.content: Apply commands only to lines matching a range or pattern.
sections.explanation: Deletes everything between START and END markers

sections.title: Multiple Commands
sections.content: Chain multiple sed commands with -e or semicolons.
sections.explanation: Replace foo with bar AND remove blank lines in one pass

common_mistakes: Forgetting /g flag — without it, only the first match per line is replaced
Using -i without a backup on important files
Special characters in patterns need escaping: . * [ \ ^ $ need \
best_practices: Test without -i first, add it only when output looks correct
Use single quotes around sed expressions to prevent shell interpretation
Combine with grep -n first to find line numbers before editing

exercises.prompt: Replace all commas with pipes in employees.csv (print to stdout)
exercises.hint: Use s/,/|/g

exercises.prompt: Remove all blank lines from server.log
exercises.hint: The pattern ^$ matches empty lines

quiz.question: What does sed 's/a/b/' do without the /g flag?
options: Replaces only the first 'a' per line
quiz.answer: Replaces only the first 'a' per line
quiz.explanation: Without /g (global), sed only replaces the first match on each line.

lessons.title: awk: Data Extraction Engine
lessons.introduction: awk is a complete programming language built for text processing. It reads input line by line, splits each line into fields, and lets you write programs that process, filter, and compute on structured text data.
lessons.purpose: Field-based text processing, filtering, arithmetic, and report generation

sections.title: Field Access
sections.content: awk splits each line by whitespace. $1 is field 1, $2 is field 2, $0 is the whole line, NF is number of fields.
sections.explanation: Prints the first field (IP address) from every line

sections.title: Custom Delimiter
sections.content: Use -F to set the field separator.
sections.explanation: Treats CSV commas as separators, prints name and salary

sections.title: Pattern Filtering
sections.content: awk programs have the form: PATTERN { ACTION }. Only run action on matching lines.
sections.explanation: Same as grep ERROR but with awk's processing power available

sections.title: Arithmetic
sections.content: awk can do math. Sum a column, compute averages, compare values.
sections.explanation: Computes average salary (skipping header row)

sections.title: NR and FNR
sections.content: NR is the total record (line) number. FNR resets per file. Useful for skipping headers with NR>1.
sections.explanation: Skips the header line and prints only employee names

sections.title: Associative Arrays
sections.content: awk has hash maps built in — perfect for grouping and counting.
sections.example: awk '{count[$1]++} END {for(ip in count) print count[ip], ip}' apache.log
sections.explanation: Counts requests per IP address — no sort|uniq needed

common_mistakes: Forgetting NR>1 to skip CSV headers
Using == for string comparison (correct) vs = for assignment
Forgetting END block for final output after processing all lines
best_practices: Use printf for formatted output: printf "%-20s %5d\n", name, count
Initialize variables explicitly: BEGIN {sum=0}
Test with NR<=5 first to see output before processing the whole file

exercises.prompt: Print only the department column (col 3) from employees.csv, skipping the header
exercises.hint: Use -F',' and NR>1

exercises.prompt: Count how many requests each HTTP status code got in apache.log
exercises.solution: awk '{count[$9]++} END {for(s in count) print count[s], s}' apache.log | sort -rn
exercises.hint: Field 9 is the status code. Use an associative array.

quiz.question: In awk, what does $0 represent?
quiz.explanation: $0 is the entire record (line). $1 is field 1, $NF is the last field, NR is the line number.

quiz.question: To set comma as the field separator in awk, use flag ___
quiz.explanation: -F sets the field separator. -F',' makes awk treat commas as delimiters.

tracks.name: Text Processing Mastery
tracks.description: Master the classic Unix text-processing toolkit.

lessons.title: cut, sort & uniq: The Classic Trio
lessons.introduction: cut, sort, and uniq are three complementary tools that together handle most data wrangling tasks. cut extracts columns, sort orders lines, and uniq deduplicates — together they form the backbone of Unix data pipelines.
lessons.purpose: Extract columns, sort data, and remove duplicates

sections.title: cut: Column Extraction
sections.content: cut extracts portions of each line. Use -d for delimiter and -f for field numbers.
sections.explanation: Extracts name (field 2) and salary (field 4) from the CSV

sections.title: cut Character Ranges
sections.content: Without -d, cut works on character positions with -c.
sections.explanation: Extracts the first 15 characters — useful for fixed-width formats

sections.title: sort: Ordering Lines
sections.content: sort arranges lines alphabetically by default. -n for numeric, -r for reverse, -k for a specific field.
sections.explanation: Sorts CSV by salary column (4) in descending numeric order

sections.title: sort Unique
sections.content: sort -u both sorts and removes duplicates in one step.
sections.explanation: Gets unique IPs without needing to pipe through uniq

sections.title: uniq: Deduplication
sections.content: uniq removes adjacent duplicate lines. Always sort first! -c adds counts, -d shows only duplicates.
sections.explanation: Counts and ranks the most repeated log messages

sections.title: The Power Pipeline
sections.content: Combining all three gives you instant data analytics.
sections.explanation: Instantly shows employee count per department, ranked

common_mistakes: Using uniq without sort first — it only removes adjacent duplicates
Forgetting -n with sort for numeric sorting (10 sorts before 9 alphabetically)
cut fields start at 1, not 0
best_practices: Prefer awk -F for CSV when fields might contain commas within quotes
sort -u is faster than sort | uniq for simple deduplication
Use -k2 -k1 with sort for secondary sort keys

exercises.prompt: List all unique departments in employees.csv (sorted alphabetically)
exercises.hint: Column 3, skip header, deduplicate

exercises.prompt: Show frequency count of each HTTP status code in apache.log, highest first
exercises.hint: Extract field 9, then sort|uniq -c|sort -rn

quiz.question: Why must you sort before using uniq?
options: uniq requires sorted input to function at all
quiz.explanation: uniq compares consecutive lines. Non-adjacent duplicates won't be caught unless the input is sorted first.

lessons.title: tr, wc, head & tail: The Utilities Belt
lessons.introduction: Four small but essential tools: tr translates characters, wc counts things, head shows the start, tail shows the end. They appear in almost every serious pipeline.
lessons.purpose: Character translation, counting, and file slicing
lessons.syntax: tr SET1 SET2  |  wc [-l|-w|-c]  |  head/tail [-n N]

sections.title: wc: Word Count
sections.content: wc counts lines (-l), words (-w), or bytes (-c).
sections.explanation: How many log entries? wc -l tells you instantly

sections.title: head: Top of File
sections.content: head shows the first N lines (default 10).
sections.explanation: Preview a log file without loading the whole thing

sections.title: tail: End of File
sections.content: tail shows the last N lines. tail -f follows new lines as they're appended (live log monitoring).
sections.explanation: See the most recent 100 log entries

sections.title: tail -n +N: Skip Lines
sections.content: tail -n +2 skips the first line — the standard way to strip CSV headers.
sections.explanation: Counts data rows excluding the header

sections.title: tr: Character Translation
sections.content: tr translates or deletes characters. Use it to change case, replace chars, or squeeze spaces.
sections.explanation: Converts lowercase to uppercase

sections.title: tr for Tokenization
sections.content: tr -s '[:space:]' '\n' splits any whitespace-separated text into one word per line.
sections.example: cat server.log | tr -s '[:space:]' '\n' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
sections.explanation: Word frequency analysis of a log file in one pipeline

common_mistakes: tail -f vs tail -n — -f follows, -n picks line count
wc -l counts newlines, so a file with no trailing newline shows one less
tr works on characters, not strings — use sed for multi-char replacement
best_practices: Use head -c 1M to preview the first megabyte of a large file
Combine: tail -n +2 file | head -100 to get lines 2–101
tr -d '\r' fixes Windows line endings (CRLF → LF)

exercises.prompt: Count the exact number of data rows in employees.csv (excluding header)
exercises.hint: Skip first line with tail -n +2, then count

exercises.prompt: Convert all text in server.log to uppercase
exercises.hint: tr can translate character ranges

quiz.question: What does tail -n +2 do?
options: Skips the first line and shows rest
Shows lines starting from line 2 onwards
quiz.answer: Skips the first line and shows rest
quiz.explanation: -n +2 means 'start from line 2' — effectively skipping line 1. Used to strip headers.

tracks.name: Regex Academy
tracks.description: Master regular expressions — the Swiss Army knife of text patterns.

lessons.title: Regular Expressions: Pattern Mastery
lessons.introduction: Regular expressions are a pattern language for describing text. They power grep, sed, awk, and virtually every programming language. Learning regex is one of the highest-leverage skills in computing — a few hours of study saves thousands of hours of manual text work.
lessons.purpose: Match, extract, and transform text using declarative patterns

sections.title: Anchors: ^ and $
sections.content: ^ matches the start of a line, $ matches the end. Anchors let you be precise about where a pattern must appear.
sections.explanation: Matches only lines that START with ERROR (not 'caught ERROR')

sections.title: Any Character: Dot
sections.content: The . (dot) matches any single character except newline.
sections.explanation: Matches ERROR, ERR0R, ERR_R — anything in position 4

sections.title: Quantifiers: * + ?
sections.content: * means zero or more, + means one or more, ? means zero or one. Apply to the preceding element.
sections.explanation: ERR+OR matches ERRRROR — one or more R's between E and OR

sections.title: Character Classes: []
sections.content: [abc] matches any one of a, b, or c. [a-z] matches any lowercase letter. [^abc] matches anything NOT in the set.
sections.explanation: Matches IP-like patterns: three digits, dot, three digits

sections.title: Alternation: |
sections.content: The | operator means OR. It matches either the left or right expression. Requires -E in grep.
sections.explanation: Matches any of three severity levels

sections.title: Grouping: ()
sections.content: Parentheses group expressions and capture for backreferences in sed.
sections.explanation: Captures ERROR and the message, rearranges the format

sections.title: Escape Sequences
sections.content: \d matches digits, \w matches word chars, \s matches whitespace — in grep -P (Perl) or awk.
sections.explanation: Extracts IPv4 addresses using Perl-compatible regex

common_mistakes: Forgetting -E for | and + — basic grep doesn't support them
Dot matches everything including literal dot — escape it: \.
Greedy matching: .* grabs as much as possible — use .*? for lazy in Perl regex
best_practices: Build patterns incrementally and test with a small file first
Use grep -P for Perl regex (\d, \w, \s) when available
Online testers like regex101.com help visualize what your pattern matches

exercises.prompt: Extract all IPv4 addresses from apache.log (one per line, deduplicated)
exercises.hint: Use grep -oE with an IP regex pattern

exercises.prompt: Find all lines in server.log that start with a timestamp (digits)
exercises.hint: Use ^ to anchor to line start, then a digit class

quiz.question: What does the regex pattern ^[0-9]+ match?
options: Lines that start with one or more digits
quiz.answer: Lines that start with one or more digits
quiz.explanation: ^ anchors to line start, [0-9] matches digits, + means one or more.

quiz.question: Which flag enables | and + in grep?
quiz.explanation: -E enables Extended Regular Expressions, which support | (OR), + (one or more), and ? (zero or one).

tracks.name: Shell Pipelines
tracks.description: Compose powerful data pipelines by chaining Unix commands.

lessons.title: Pipelines: The Unix Philosophy
lessons.introduction: The pipe (|) is one of the most powerful ideas in computing: take the output of one command and feed it as input to another. This lets you compose small, focused tools into powerful data processing pipelines — no programming required.
lessons.purpose: Chain commands so stdout of one becomes stdin of the next

sections.title: The Pipe Operator
sections.content: | passes stdout of the left command to stdin of the right. Commands run concurrently — data streams through.
sections.explanation: Count error lines: read file → filter errors → count

sections.title: Building Step by Step
sections.content: Always build pipelines incrementally. Run each step, verify output, add the next.
sections.example: awk '{print $1}' apache.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
sections.explanation: Top 10 IPs: extract → sort → count → re-sort → limit

sections.title: Redirection: > and >>
sections.content: > redirects output to a file (overwrites). >> appends. < reads stdin from a file.
sections.explanation: Save all error lines to a separate file for later analysis

sections.title: tee: Split the Stream
sections.content: tee writes to both stdout AND a file simultaneously. See output while saving it.
sections.example: grep ERROR server.log | tee errors.txt | wc -l
sections.explanation: Saves errors to file AND prints the count to terminal

sections.title: xargs: Distribute Arguments
sections.content: xargs takes stdin and passes items as arguments to another command.
sections.explanation: Count lines in every log file that contains errors

common_mistakes: Piping to a command that doesn't read stdin (use xargs instead)
Overwriting a file you're reading: cat file | grep x > file empties it!
Missing quotes around patterns with spaces or special characters
best_practices: Build and test each stage before adding the next
Use tee to save intermediate results while debugging
Document complex pipelines with comments in a script

exercises.prompt: Count how many unique IPs are in apache.log
exercises.hint: Extract IPs, deduplicate, then count

exercises.prompt: Show the 5 most common words in server.log
exercises.solution: cat server.log | tr -s '[:space:]' '\n' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -5
exercises.hint: Split to words, sort, count, re-sort, limit to 5

quiz.question: What does the > operator do?
quiz.explanation: > redirects stdout to a file, overwriting it. >> appends. | pipes to another command. < reads from file.

lessons.title: find & xargs: Filesystem Mastery
lessons.introduction: find and xargs together let you locate files by any criteria and apply operations to them in bulk — one of the most powerful combinations in the Unix toolbox.
lessons.purpose: Locate files and apply bulk operations using find and xargs

sections.title: find by Name
sections.content: Find files matching a name pattern anywhere in the directory tree.
sections.explanation: Recursively finds all .log files from current directory

sections.title: find by Type
sections.content: -type f for files, -type d for directories.
sections.explanation: Only files named *.csv, not directories

sections.title: find by Size
sections.content: -size finds files above/below a size. +10M means over 10 megabytes.
sections.explanation: Find all files over 1MB — useful for disk cleanup

sections.title: find by Time
sections.content: -mtime -1 means modified in the last day. -newer FILE means newer than a reference file.
sections.explanation: Find log files modified today

sections.title: xargs: Batch Processing
sections.content: xargs converts stdin lines to arguments for another command.
sections.explanation: Count lines in every log file found

sections.title: xargs with Spaces
sections.content: Use -print0 with find and -0 with xargs to handle filenames with spaces.
sections.explanation: Safely process log files even if they have spaces in names

common_mistakes: Using find without -maxdepth on huge directory trees
Forgetting -print0 / -0 for filenames with spaces
find . -name *.log without quotes — shell expands * before find sees it
best_practices: Always quote patterns: find . -name '*.log' not *.log
Use find ... -exec cmd {} + for performance (batches args like xargs)
Add -maxdepth 2 to limit recursion depth on large trees

exercises.prompt: Find all files in the workspace directory and count how many there are
exercises.hint: find -type f lists files, pipe to wc -l

quiz.question: What does find . -mtime -1 find?
options: Files modified in the last 24 hours
quiz.answer: Files modified in the last 24 hours
quiz.explanation: -mtime -1 means 'modified less than 1 day ago' — the minus sign means 'less than N days'.

tracks.name: Log Analysis
tracks.description: Real-world log parsing: Apache, Nginx, syslog, and application logs.

lessons.title: Log Analysis: Reading System Stories
lessons.introduction: Logs are the heartbeat of every system. Learning to parse them with command-line tools is a superpower — you can diagnose incidents, find security issues, and understand system behavior without any specialized tools.
lessons.purpose: Parse Apache, Nginx, syslog, and custom application logs
lessons.syntax: grep | awk | sed | sort | uniq applied to structured log files

sections.title: Apache Combined Log Format
sections.content: Apache logs follow: IP - - [datetime] "METHOD URL protocol" status bytes "referer" "user-agent"
sections.explanation: Extracts IP, URL, and status code from the first 5 lines

sections.title: Counting Status Codes
sections.content: Quick health check: what response codes is your server sending?
sections.explanation: Distribution of all HTTP status codes — 200s are good, 500s are errors

sections.title: Traffic Analysis
sections.content: Find peak traffic periods and most popular content.
sections.explanation: Request count per hour of day

sections.title: Error Investigation
sections.content: Find and count 404 errors — missing pages that need fixing.
sections.example: awk '$9==404 {print $7}' apache.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
sections.explanation: Top 10 missing URLs — could be a broken link or a probing attack

sections.title: syslog Format
sections.content: syslog format: Month Day HH:MM:SS hostname process[pid]: message
sections.explanation: Error frequency by service

sections.title: Bandwidth Analysis
sections.content: Calculate total bytes transferred from Apache log field 10.
sections.explanation: Total bandwidth served — useful for billing and capacity planning

common_mistakes: Forgetting that Apache field numbers shift if your log format differs
Treating IP::port as just IP without extracting the IP part
Not handling log rotation — yesterday's log is in a .1 or .gz file
best_practices: Build a library of one-liners for your most common queries
Use awk BEGIN to print headers for your reports
Cross-reference multiple log files to correlate events

exercises.prompt: Find the top 3 most requested URLs in apache.log
exercises.solution: awk '{print $7}' apache.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -3
exercises.hint: Field 7 is the URL, then sort|uniq|sort|head

exercises.prompt: Count total 500 errors in apache.log
exercises.hint: Filter where field 9 equals 500, then count

quiz.question: In Apache Combined Log Format, which field contains the HTTP status code?
quiz.explanation: The standard Apache Combined Log Format: IP - - [timestamp] "request" STATUS bytes "referer" "user-agent". Status is field 9.

lessons.title: Advanced Log Analysis: Incident Response
lessons.introduction: When systems go wrong, the logs tell the story. Advanced log analysis combines pattern matching, time correlation, and multi-file analysis to diagnose incidents in minutes rather than hours.
lessons.purpose: Correlate events across log files and detect anomalies

sections.title: Time Windowing
sections.content: Extract events in a specific time window using awk comparisons on the timestamp field.
sections.explanation: Count requests during the 14:00–14:59 hour (2 PM)

sections.title: Anomaly Detection
sections.content: Spot IPs with unusually high request rates — a sign of bots or attacks.
sections.example: awk '{count[$1]++} END {for(ip in count) if(count[ip]>100) print ip, count[ip]}' apache.log
sections.explanation: Lists any IP with over 100 requests — potential threat

sections.title: Error Rate Trending
sections.content: Calculate error rates over time to spot degradation.
sections.example: awk '{total++; if($9>=500) errors++} END {printf "Error rate: %.1f%%\n", errors/total*100}' apache.log
sections.explanation: Overall 5xx error rate — the key SLO metric

sections.title: Multi-Log Correlation
sections.content: Combine apache and nginx logs for a unified view.
sections.example: cat apache.log nginx.log | sort -k4 | grep 'ERROR\|500' | tail -50
sections.explanation: Unified error timeline across both web servers

sections.title: Failed Login Detection
sections.content: Find repeated authentication failures — brute force attacks leave a pattern.
sections.example: grep 'authentication failure\|Failed password' syslog.log | awk '{print $NF}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
sections.explanation: Top 10 users targeted by auth failures

sections.title: Reporting with awk
sections.content: Generate a proper incident report with awk's printf.
sections.example: awk '{errors[$9]++} END {print "=== Error Report ==="; for(s in errors) if(s>=400) printf "%s: %d\n", s, errors[s]}' apache.log | sort
sections.explanation: Formatted error report showing all HTTP error codes and counts

common_mistakes: Looking at logs in isolation — always correlate with other log sources
Forgetting timezone differences when correlating logs from different systems
Sampling logs without understanding sampling bias
best_practices: Archive raw logs before analysis — you may need to re-examine them
Save your one-liners to a runbook for the next incident
Always check log file sizes before cat-ing — use head first

exercises.prompt: Calculate the 5xx error rate as a percentage in apache.log
exercises.solution: awk '{t++; if($9>=500) e++} END {printf "%.1f%%\n", e/t*100}' apache.log
exercises.hint: Count total and errors separately, compute percentage in END

quiz.question: You want to find IPs making more than 50 requests. Where does the filter go in awk?
options: In an END block after counting
quiz.answer: In an END block after counting
quiz.explanation: You must count ALL requests per IP first (using an array), then filter in END where totals are available.

tracks.name: VLSI Text Processing
tracks.description: Parse VLSI EDA tool reports: timing, synthesis, liberty, and constraints.

lessons.title: VLSI Reports: EDA Text Processing
lessons.introduction: VLSI design tools generate massive text reports. Physical design engineers spend significant time parsing timing reports, synthesis logs, and liberty files. Command-line tools turn hours of manual searching into seconds of automated extraction.
lessons.purpose: Parse EDA tool outputs: timing reports, synthesis logs, SDC, Liberty
lessons.syntax: grep | awk | sed applied to EDA report formats

sections.title: Timing Reports
sections.content: Timing reports show setup/hold slack for each path. Negative slack = violation.
sections.explanation: Quick count of timing violations — the number every PE watches

sections.title: Worst Negative Slack
sections.content: Find the critical path — the path with the worst (most negative) slack.
sections.explanation: Extracts the WNS — the number that defines your achievable frequency

sections.title: Liberty File Parsing
sections.content: Liberty files define cell timing and power. Counting cells, pins, and attributes.
sections.explanation: Total standard cells in the library

sections.title: SDC Constraint Analysis
sections.content: SDC files define timing constraints. Extract clock definitions and frequencies.
sections.explanation: Extracts all clock periods (divide 1000 to get MHz)

sections.title: Synthesis Report Mining
sections.content: Synthesis logs contain area, power, and QoR summaries buried in verbose output.
sections.explanation: Extracts the cell area section from synthesis report

sections.title: Netlist Statistics
sections.content: Count cells, instances, and connections in a Verilog netlist.
sections.explanation: Count module port connections — proxy for design size

common_mistakes: EDA reports often have leading whitespace — use \s* or trim before comparing
Numbers may be in scientific notation (1.23e-09) — handle with awk
Report format changes between tool versions — always verify column positions
best_practices: Build a library of EDA parsing scripts versioned with your design
Use awk for multi-field extraction from tabular EDA reports
Cross-check extracted numbers against the tool's own summary report

exercises.prompt: Count the number of timing violations (VIOLATED) in timing.rpt
exercises.hint: grep -c counts matching lines

exercises.prompt: List all clock names defined in constraints.sdc
exercises.hint: Look for -name argument in create_clock statements

quiz.question: What does negative slack in a timing report mean?
options: The path has a timing violation
quiz.answer: The path has a timing violation
quiz.explanation: Negative slack means the signal arrives late — the timing constraint is violated. The design cannot run at the target frequency.
